claimed_contribution: The paper explicitly distinguishes presales service and after-sales support and models their interdependence, where presales effort reduces the probability of after-sales support demand.
It studies channel service design by endogenizing which channel member provides presales service and after-sales support, instead of assuming one provider handles service.
It adds a manufacturer-retailer presales cost-sharing extension and shows that cost sharing can hurt the retailer and even reduce total channel profit.
context: The setting is a manufacturer-retailer distribution channel for information-intensive complex durable goods (for example, consumer electronics and home appliances) where consumers make a one-time purchase. Consumers may need postpurchase usage help, and support can come from the retailer, the manufacturer, or third-party providers.
design_and_data: The paper is a theoretical game-theoretic model with one manufacturer, one retailer, and consumers with valuations drawn from U[0,1]. Presales service by manufacturer and/or retailer lowers consumers' probability of needing after-sales support; presales has convex fixed investment costs, while after-sales support has variable handling cost plus a fixed effectiveness cost. The manufacturer first chooses wholesale price, presales effort, and whether to invest in after-sales support; the retailer then chooses retail price, presales effort, and possibly after-sales support, and subgame-perfect equilibria are derived analytically. Robustness extensions cover noncoprovision of presales service, asymmetric service costs, retail competition, alternative functional forms, and presales cost sharing by the manufacturer.
focal_constructs: fixed cost of effective after-sales support
main_findings: In equilibrium of the main model, both the manufacturer and the retailer provide strictly positive presales service.

Who provides after-sales support is threshold-driven by the fixed effectiveness cost: the retailer provides it when the fixed cost is low, the manufacturer may provide it at intermediate levels, and neither provides it when the fixed cost is high.
When the retailer provides after-sales support, the highest presales service and the highest demand for after-sales support can occur simultaneously under stated parameter conditions.
one_line_summary: The article develops a distribution-channel game showing how fixed support costs govern whether retailer, manufacturer, or neither provides after-sales support, while both parties still provide presales service and retailer-led support can yield both maximal presales effort and maximal support demand.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior channel-service models by separating presales service from after-sales support and linking them through consumers' expected postpurchase inconvenience. It introduces an endogenous service-channel-design problem over who provides each service, whereas much prior work treats service as a single input or assumes one provider. It challenges standard coordination intuition by showing manufacturer presales cost sharing can reduce retailer and total channel profits in some configurations.
research_gap: Prior channel-service literature often treats service as a single nonprice input and usually assumes one channel member provides it. The paper identifies a missing treatment of distinct but linked presales and after-sales services, and a missing analysis of endogenous service-provider choice in the channel.
research_question: How do a manufacturer and a retailer choose presales service, after-sales support provision, and prices when consumers anticipate postpurchase inconvenience from using complex products? Which service configuration emerges in equilibrium as after-sales fixed costs vary, and what does that imply for presales effort and after-sales support demand?
